children.name: Vireo
children.description: Typical vireos — small, insectivorous songbirds of the Americas. Their name derives from Latin for 'I am green' — most species are olive-green above and pale below. Vireos are deliberate foragers, carefully inspecting leaves and branches for caterpillars and insects. They are better known for their persistent, repetitive songs than their subdued plumage.

children.name: Vireo olivaceus
children.description: One of the most common songbirds of North American deciduous forests. Olive-green above, white below, with a striking red eye and a grey crown bordered by black. Its persistent, monotonous song — a series of short phrases separated by pauses — can continue all day in summer. Longest song of any North American bird.

children.name: Vireo flavifrons
children.description: A striking vireo with a bright yellow throat and breast, white wing bars, and yellow spectacles. Found in open deciduous woodlands of eastern North America. Its song is a slow, burry series of three-note phrases.

children.name: Vireo gilvus
children.description: A plain, greyish vireo with no wing bars and a pale supercilium. Its song is a cheerful, rolling warble that seems to run on for ever. Found in open deciduous and riparian woodlands. It was the first bird ever recorded to lay a blue egg.

children.name: Vireo bellii
children.description: A small, greyish vireo of shrubby habitats in the central and southwestern United States and Mexico. One of the plainest vireos, lacking wing bars. Its song is a rapid, jumbled series of notes. The Least Bell's Vireo subspecies is Endangered.

children.name: Vireo griseus
children.description: A distinctive vireo with a white eye, yellow spectacles, and yellow flanks. Found in dense thickets and scrubby habitats in the eastern United States. Its explosive song begins with a sharp 'chick' before breaking into a complex series of notes. More often heard than seen.

children.name: Cyclarhis
children.description: Peppershrikes — stocky, large-billed vireos of Central and South America. Named for their preference for eating pepper berries and other fruit, which they crush with their thick, hooked bills. Their loud, ringing songs are a characteristic sound of Neotropical forest edges.

children.name: Cyclarhis gujanensis
children.description: A distinctive vireonid with a bold rufous eyebrow, grey crown, and yellow throat. Found from Mexico to Argentina. Its loud, ringing song of three to four notes is one of the most characteristic sounds of Neotropical forests. Feeds on fruit and large insects.

children.name: Vireolanius
children.description: Shrike-vireos — a genus of stocky, large-billed vireos with shrike-like proportions and striking facial patterns. They are slower-moving than typical vireos and often remain motionless for long periods while foraging. They are found in the forests of Central and South America.

children.name: Vireolanius eximius
children.description: A striking shrike-vireo with a bright yellow eyebrow, grey crown, and olive-green upperparts, found in lowland forests of northern South America. It has a stout, hooked bill used to capture large insects. Its song is a series of slow, clear whistles.

children.name: Vireolanius leucotis
children.description: The slaty-capped shrike-vireo has a grey crown, olive-green upperparts, yellow throat, and white underparts with yellow flanks. It ranges across the Amazon basin in lowland rainforests. It forages slowly and deliberately in the canopy.

children.name: Vireolanius melitophrys
children.description: The chestnut-sided shrike-vireo has a grey crown, bright chestnut flanks, and olive-green upperparts, found in the highlands of southern Mexico and Guatemala. It inhabits humid montane forests and cloud forests. Its song is a series of loud, ringing whistles.

children.name: Vireolanius pulchellus
children.description: The green shrike-vireo is a bright olive-green bird with yellow underparts and a thick, hooked bill. It is found from southern Mexico to Panama in lowland and foothill forests. It is a slow, deliberate forager that often joins mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Hylophilus
children.description: Greenlets — a genus of small, olive-green vireos found in the forests of Central and South America. They are active, acrobatic foragers that glean insects from foliage in the canopy and understory. Their songs are typically high-pitched and insect-like.

children.name: Hylophilus flavipes
children.description: A small greenlet with olive-green upperparts, a grey crown, and yellow underparts, found in northern South America in dry forests and scrub. It is an active forager, constantly flitting through the foliage. Its song is a high-pitched, rapid series of notes.

children.name: Hylophilus pectoralis
children.description: The ashy-headed greenlet has a distinctive grey head, olive-green upperparts, and yellowish underparts. It is found in lowland forests of northern South America. It forages actively in the canopy, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Hylophilus amaurocephalus
children.description: The gray-eyed greenlet has olive-green upperparts, a grey crown, and yellowish underparts with a distinctive grey eye. It is found in the Caatinga and Cerrado regions of eastern Brazil. It inhabits dry forests and woodlands.

children.name: Hylophilus hypoxanthus
children.description: The dusky-capped greenlet has a dark grey cap, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. It is found across the Amazon basin in lowland rainforests. It forages actively in the canopy, gleaning insects from leaves.

children.name: Hylophilus muscicapinus
children.description: The buff-cheeked greenlet has olive-green upperparts, a grey crown, and a distinctive buff-coloured cheek patch. It is found in the Guiana Shield region of northern South America. It inhabits lowland rainforests and forages in the subcanopy.

children.name: Hylophilus thoracicus
children.description: The lemon-chested greenlet has a bright lemon-yellow breast, olive-green upperparts, and a grey crown. It is found across the Amazon basin and the Guiana Shield. It inhabits lowland rainforests and forages in the canopy.

children.name: Tunchiornis
children.description: A genus of greenlet with a distinctive tawny crown, found in the lowland forests of Central and South America. It was formerly included in Hylophilus but is now recognised as a distinct genus based on genetic studies.

children.name: Tunchiornis ochraceiceps
children.description: The tawny-crowned greenlet has a distinctive tawny crown, olive-green upperparts, and pale grey underparts. It ranges from Honduras through Central America to the Amazon basin. It inhabits lowland rainforests and forages actively in the canopy.

children.name: Pachysylvia
children.description: A genus of greenlets with relatively stout bills, found in the forests of Central and South America. Like other greenlets, they are active insectivores that forage in the canopy and subcanopy, often with mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Pachysylvia olivacea
children.description: The olivaceous greenlet is a uniform olive-green bird with a slightly paler belly and a faint pale eye-ring. It is found in the lowland forests of the Guiana Shield and northern Amazon. It forages actively in the canopy for insects.

children.name: Pachysylvia decurtata
children.description: The lesser greenlet is a small, olive-green bird with pale yellowish underparts and a short tail. It ranges from Belize through Central America to Bolivia and Brazil. It inhabits lowland forests and forest edges, foraging actively in the canopy.

children.name: Pachysylvia aurantiifrons
children.description: The golden-fronted greenlet has a distinctive golden-yellow forehead and crown, olive-green upperparts, and yellow underparts. It is found from Panama through northern South America. It inhabits lowland forests and forest edges, often joining mixed-species flocks.
